Ten years after the Paris Agreement, global temperatures are dangerously close to the 1.5°C threshold, at 1.45°C above pre-industrial levels in 2024, according to the IPCC's Sixth Assessment Report.

In 2023, global CO2 emissions reached a record high of 37.4 gigatonnes, despite growth in renewable energy; fossil fuel use remains high, with coal consumption in South and Southeast Asia offsetting other gains, as noted by the IEA.

The European Union has reduced emissions by 32% since 1990 through its European Green Deal, but Brazil faces criticism for rapid deforestation in the Amazon under President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva, with 11,568 km² lost in 2024 alone.

India, the world's second-largest emitter after China and the US, has pledged to be climate neutral by 2070, a timeline criticized as too late by activists like Greta Thunberg and the Youth Climate Coalition.

A September 2025 InfluenceMap report revealed that major oil and gas companies spent $750 million on lobbying against climate legislation between 2020-2024, with Shell's Wael Sawan dismissing the findings as selective and misleading.

The upcoming COP30 in Belém, Brazil, scheduled for November 2025, will test international resolve; UN Secretary-General António Guterres has called for updated Nationally Determined Contributions with more ambitious 2035 goals.

Despite falling short of required targets, the renewable energy sector offers hope, with solar capacity surpassing 2 terawatts in 2024 and investments by Ørsted and Iberdrola reaching records; battery storage costs have dropped 89% since 2010.
Ten years after the Paris Agreement was signed in 2015, global temperatures reached 1.45°C above pre-industrial levels in 2024.
Major fossil fuel companies spent $750 million lobbying against climate legislation between 2020 and 2024, while Brazil faces criticism for its Amazon deforestation rates.
Renewable energy growth offers hope, with solar capacity surpassing 2 terawatts globally in 2024, but updated commitments at COP30 in 2025 are essential for meeting climate goals.
The Paris Agreement, formally adopted on December 12, 2015, aimed to limit global warming to 1.5°C, but in 2024, temperatures reached 1.45°C above pre-industrial levels as reported by the IPCC.
Global CO₂ emissions peaked at 37.4 gigaton in 2023, with the EU reducing its emissions by 32% since 1990, while Brazil faced criticism for deforestation of 11,568 km² in 2024.

Fossil fuel lobbyists spent $750 million opposing climate legislation between 2020 and 2024, yet renewable energy advancements, like solar capacity exceeding 2 terawatts, offer hope for meeting climate goals.

The upcoming COP30 conference in November 2025 will assess international commitment to revised climate targets by 2035, emphasizing the need for political will as highlighted by figures like Katharine Hayhoe.
